function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---

results.content:
Based on the provided MD&A and financial disclosures, here is the confirmation of SpaceX's Connectivity segment revenue for Q1 2026 and the context regarding its composition:

**Connectivity Segment Revenue (Q1 2026)**
For the three months ended March 31, 2026, SpaceX's Connectivity segment generated **$3,257 million** in revenue. This represents an increase of $782 million, or 31.6%, compared to $2,475 million in the prior year quarter (Q1 2025). 

**Segment Composition**
Yes, the Connectivity segment includes Starlink consumer, enterprise, and government services. Specifically, the segment generates revenue from the following:
*   **Consumer:** Revenue is driven primarily by monthly subscription fees for broadband services (based on geographic market and download speed) and the one-time sale of Starlink Kits (inclusive of the terminal). 
*   **Enterprise:** Revenue is generated through contracts structured as a combination of subscriptions, data consumption, and capacity. This includes aviation, maritime, land mobility, and other enterprise business across various industries.
*   **Government:** Revenue is generated via long-term contracts for Starshield, a secure satellite network designed specifically for government use. 
*   **Starlink Mobile:** The segment also includes mobile connectivity services, which are accounted for under Enterprise & Government revenue. 

**Q1 2026 Performance Context**
The 31.6% year-over-year revenue growth was driven by:
*   **Consumer Revenue:** Increased by $656 million, driven by 104.7% growth in Starlink Subscribers. This was partially offset by a 22.9% decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU, primarily due to international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans.
*   **Enterprise & Government Revenue:** Increased by $126 million. This was driven by $209 million in growth from aviation, maritime, and other enterprise business, plus $85 million from mobile connectivity. This was partially offset by a $175 million decrease in the government connectivity business.

Despite rising costs, the segment's profitability improved. Connectivity income from operations increased by $155 million (15.0%) to $1,188 million, and Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA increased by $469 million to $2,087 million. Cost of revenue rose by $437 million (36.0%) primarily due to higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs, alongside increased ground operating, customer support, and international expansion expenses. R&D also grew by $82 million (66.7%) for next-generation satellite and Starlink Kit development.

## Implied Revenue Multiple: Starlink vs. OneWeb

### Source Figures

**SpaceX (S-1, Q1 2026 — three months ended March 31, 2026):**
- Connectivity segment revenue: **$3,257 million**
- (Up 31.6% YoY from $2,475M in Q1 2025; includes Starlink consumer subscriptions, Starlink Kit hardware sales, enterprise [aviation/maritime/land mobility], government [Starshield], and mobile connectivity)

**Eutelsat (H1 FY2025-26 — six months ended December 31, 2025):**
- LEO (OneWeb) revenue: **€110.5 million**
- (Up ~59.7% like-for-like YoY; represents ~20% of group revenue and over one-third of Connectivity revenues)

### Annualization to a Common Calendar-Year Basis

| Metric | Reported | Annualization Factor | Annualized Run-Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| SpaceX Connectivity (Q1 2026) | $3,257M | × 4 | **$13,028M** |
| Eutelsat LEO/OneWeb (H1 FY25-26) | €110.5M | × 2 | **€221.0M** |

### Currency Conversion (EUR → USD)
Using the EUR/USD spot rate at the close of Eutelsat's reporting period (December 31, 2025 = 1.1746):
- €221.0M × 1.1746 = **~$259.59M**

(Using the early-March 2026 rate of ~1.1759 yields ~$259.88M — immaterially different.)

### Implied Revenue Multiple

**$13,028M ÷ $259.59M ≈ 50.19x**

Equivalently, converting SpaceX to EUR: (€2,774.1M × 4) ÷ €221M ≈ **50.19x**

**Starlink's annualized run-rate exceeds OneWeb's by approximately 50x.**

---

### What This Reveals About Relative Commercial Maturity

1. **Order-of-magnitude scale gap.** Starlink's connectivity business is generating revenue at roughly fifty times the rate of OneWeb. Even though OneWeb is growing faster in percentage terms (~60% vs. ~32%), the absolute dollar gap is widening, not narrowing. Starlink added ~$782M of incremental YoY quarterly revenue; OneWeb added roughly €41M of incremental semi-annual revenue (~$48M) — meaning Starlink is adding connectivity revenue at a pace roughly 30–40x faster in absolute terms.

2. **Direct-to-consumer vs. wholesale architecture.** Starlink's scale reflects a vertically integrated, direct-to-consumer model (subscriptions + terminal hardware sales to millions of subscribers), whereas OneWeb operates primarily as a wholesale/B2B capacity provider selling to telecom partners, ISPs, governments, and enterprises. The DTC model produces far higher revenue per unit of constellation capacity because it monetizes the end-user directly rather than sharing economics with distribution partners.

3. **Different points on the maturity curve.** OneWeb is still in early commercial ramp (constellation only recently completed, ~20% of group revenue, capacity-based contracts being filled). Starlink is past the inflection: it has crossed into positive segment operating income ($1,188M in Q1 2026) and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087M, indicating the business is now self-funding its growth. OneWeb's contribution to Eutelsat's adjusted EBITDA margin actually compressed group margin (52.1% vs. 55.2%) due to product-mix effects during the LEO ramp.

4. **Subscriber economics vs. capacity economics.** SpaceX discloses Starlink Subscriber growth of 104.7% YoY (offset by a 22.9% ARPU decline from international/low-price-plan mix). Eutelsat's LEO disclosure is capacity- and contract-oriented rather than subscriber-count-oriented, reflecting the wholesale model. The two are not directly comparable on a per-customer basis.

---

### Disclosure Differences That Limit Precision of a Direct Comparison

1. **Segment scope mismatch.** SpaceX's "Connectivity" segment is broad — it bundles consumer subscriptions, terminal hardware sales, enterprise mobility, government (Starshield), and mobile. Eutelsat's "LEO" line is narrower — it is specifically OneWeb constellation revenue, which is itself a subset of Eutelsat's broader "Connectivity" revenues (which also include €132.1M of GEO Fixed Connectivity). A like-for-like comparison would require stripping hardware revenue from SpaceX and adding GEO fixed connectivity to Eutelsat, neither of which is cleanly separable from the filings.

2. **Fiscal-year and reporting-period misalignment.** SpaceX reports on calendar quarters (Q1 2026 = Jan–Mar 2026). Eutelsat reports on a July–June fiscal year (H1 FY2025-26 = Jul–Dec 2025). The two annualized figures therefore cover overlapping but non-identical 12-month windows, and neither captures identical seasonality.

3. **Currency.** SpaceX reports in USD; Eutelsat in EUR. The ~50x multiple is robust to reasonable FX assumptions (1.17–1.18 EUR/USD), but precise comparison requires a stated conversion rate and date, which neither filing prescribes for cross-company benchmarking.

4. **Annualization methodology.** Simple ×4 / ×2 multiplication assumes a flat run-rate with no intra-year growth or seasonality. Both businesses are in high-growth phases, so the annualized figures likely understate full-year actuals — and the understatement is asymmetric (Starlink's quarterly sequential growth trajectory is steeper in absolute dollars). The "multiple" is therefore a point-in-time run-rate snapshot, not a forecast of full-year revenue.

5. **Hardware vs. service revenue split.** SpaceX's Connectivity includes one-time Starlink Kit (terminal) sales, which are not separately disclosed within the segment. Eutelsat's LEO revenue presentation does not cleanly separate hardware from service. Because hardware revenue has different unit economics and recurrence than subscription revenue, blending them complicates margin and run-rate comparisons.

6. **Recast consolidated reporting (SpaceX).** SpaceX's S-1 financials are retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI Mar 28, 2025) for all historical periods. The Connectivity segment itself is SpaceX-specific and is not contaminated by the xAI/X recast, but a reader must verify that the segment figures reflect standalone SpaceX connectivity economics rather than consolidated-group allocations.

7. **Growth-rate basis.** Eutelsat's ~60% is "like-for-like" (constant scope/perimeter). SpaceX's 31.6% is reported YoY on a recast basis. The two growth rates are not computed on identical methodological footings.

8. **Subscriber/capacity disclosure asymmetry.** SpaceX discloses Starlink Subscriber counts and ARPU; Eutelsat discloses LEO revenue and capacity/contract milestones but not a directly comparable end-user subscriber count, making per-customer or per-unit-capacity benchmarking impossible from the filings alone.

---

### Bottom Line

On a like-for-like annualized run-rate basis, **Starlink's connectivity revenue is approximately 50x OneWeb's LEO revenue** (~$13.0B vs. ~$0.26B). The gap reflects not just scale but fundamentally different commercial architectures (DTC + hardware vs. wholesale capacity), different points on the maturity curve (Starlink is segment-EBITDA-positive and self-funding; OneWeb is still in early ramp and dilutive to group margin), and disclosure frameworks that are not directly reconcilable without adjustment. The ~50x figure should be read as an order-of-magnitude indicator rather than a precise like-for-like ratio.
